7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

8	/-!
9	# Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve (A.1.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.1.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the line-bundle
13	pullback functor `π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_S T)` along the projection
14	`π_T : C ×_S T → T` of a relative curve, and the set-valued relative Picard
15	presheaf
16	
17	```
18	Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)
19	```
20	
21	as a functor on `(Sch/S)^op`.
22	
23	## Status (iter-174 Lane E file-skeleton)
24	
25	This file is the **iter-174 Lane E** file-skeleton: each of the five pinned
26	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
27	blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/Picard_LineBundlePullback.tex`) with a
28	`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-175+ work after the sibling chapters
29	`Picard_RelativeSpec.lean` (A.1.a) settles its `QcohAlgebra` body, and after
30	`Picard_RelPicFunctor.lean` (A.1.c) lands the étale-sheafification overlay.
31	
32	The 5 pinned declarations are:
33	
34	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct` (def, ~5 LOC) — the type of
35	   line bundles on the product `C ×_S T`.
36	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection`
37	   (noncomputable def, ~6 LOC) — the pullback map `Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_S T)`.
38	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
39	   — composition of pullbacks `g_C^* ∘ π_T^* = π_{T'}^* ∘ g^*` (Stacks 01HG).
40	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup` (def, ~8 LOC)
41	   — well-definedness of the quotient `Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`, encoded as
42	   the substantive `Setoid` on `OnProduct πC πT` that defines the equivalence
43	   relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ (L')⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`.
44	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial`
45	   (noncomputable def, ~10 LOC) — for `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, the induced map
46	   `Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) → Pic^♯_{C/S}(T')` factoring `g_C^*` through the quotient.
47	
48	## Note on type expressivity
49	
50	Because Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `Module.Invertible`/`IsInvertible` predicate
51	on `Scheme.Modules` (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for `R : CommRing`),
52	the type `OnProduct` packaging *line bundles* (= invertible sheaves of
53	`O`-modules) on the product cannot yet be carved out of `Scheme.Modules` as a
54	subtype. Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof"
55	we encode `OnProduct` by a typed `sorry` at the type level: the iter-175+ body
56	will instantiate it as a structure pairing a `(pullback πC πT).Modules` carrier
57	with an `IsInvertible` witness once that predicate is in Mathlib (or proven
58	internally as the project-side definition).
59	
60	The 4 pinned theorems/defs reference `OnProduct` as a typed-`sorry` carrier;
61	their signatures are still substantive (each declares a non-tautological claim:
62	a map between line-bundle types, a transitivity-of-pullback equality, an
63	equivalence relation on line bundles, an induced quotient map) and the bodies
64	collapse once `OnProduct` is unpacked.
65

70	Definitions `df:aPf` (absolute Picard functor) and `df:Pfs` (relative Picard
71	functor). Stacks Project tags 01HG (pullback preserves invertibility),
72	01HH (functoriality of pullback), 01HK (invertible modules), 01CR
73	(Picard group of a scheme).

88	/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product
89	
90	A *line bundle* on the relative curve `C ×_S T` is an invertible
91	`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
92	Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
93	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
94	predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
95	in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). The iter-174 file-skeleton therefore
96	encodes the type of line bundles on the product by a typed `sorry` at the type
97	level; iter-175+ will instantiate it as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules`
98	cutting out invertibility.
99	
100	Blueprint reference: `def:line_bundle_on_product` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
101	functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/
102	
103	/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: invertible sheaves of
104	`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
105	projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
106	and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
107	Picard functor at `T`.
108	
109	iter-175+: instantiate as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules` carrying an
110	invertibility witness — concretely either
111	```
112	structure OnProduct ... where
113	  sheaf : (pullback πC πT).Modules
114	  isInvertible : IsInvertible sheaf
115	```
116	or the quotient by isomorphism for the Picard *group* refinement. For the
117	iter-174 file-skeleton this is a typed `sorry` at the type level because
118	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on `Scheme.Modules`. -/
119	noncomputable def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (_πC : C ⟶ S) (_πT : T ⟶ S) :
120	    Type (u+1) :=
121	  sorry

123	/-! ## §2. The pullback functor along the projection
124	
125	For a morphism of schemes `f : Y ⟶ Z` and an invertible `O_Z`-module `M`, the
126	pullback `f^* M` is an invertible `O_Y`-module (the pullback of an
127	open-cover-trivialisation of `M` trivialises `f^* M`, and the pullback of a free
128	module of rank one is free of rank one — Stacks tag 01HH). Specialising to the
129	projection `π_T = pullback.snd πC πT : C ×_S T ⟶ T` gives the pullback map
130	`π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_S T)`. Mathlib's underlying `Scheme.Modules.pullback`
131	functor (in `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`) supplies the pullback on
132	the full category of sheaves of modules; iter-175+ will restrict it to
133	invertible sheaves once the `OnProduct` carrier is unpacked.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:pullback_along_projection` (Kleiman §2; Stacks tag
136	01HH). -/
137	
138	/-- **Pullback along the projection** `π_T : C ×_S T ⟶ T` of a relative
139	curve. Sends an `O_T`-line bundle `N` to the `O_(C ×_S T)`-line bundle
140	`π_T^* N`, induced by the underlying `Scheme.Modules.pullback` functor on
141	`Scheme.Modules`.
142	
143	iter-175+: instantiate as `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`
144	wrapped with the carried `IsInvertible` witness (the pullback of an invertible
145	sheaf is invertible, Stacks 01HH). For the iter-174 file-skeleton the body is
146	a typed `sorry` because the carrier type `OnProduct` is itself a typed `sorry`
147	(see §1). The signature is non-tautological: it asserts a *function*
148	`T.Modules → OnProduct πC πT`, which after unfolding `OnProduct` becomes the
149	concrete pullback functor restricted to invertible modules. -/
150	noncomputable def pullbackAlongProjection {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
151	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (_N : T.Modules) : OnProduct πC πT :=
152	  sorry
153	
154	/-! ## §3. Composition of pullbacks
155	
156	The composition law for line-bundle pullback (Stacks tag 01HG) says that for any
157	composable morphisms `f : X ⟶ Y, g : Y ⟶ Z` and any line bundle `M ∈ Pic(Z)`,
158	the natural isomorphism `(g ∘ f)^* M ≅ f^* g^* M` makes the pullback functor
159	into a (pseudo-)functor `Sch^op ⥤ Cat`.
160	
161	Specialised to the relative curve setting: given `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, the
162	base-change morphism `g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T` satisfies
163	`π_T ∘ g_C = g ∘ π_{T'}` (definition of the fiber product). Therefore on
164	line bundles `g_C^* ∘ π_T^* = π_{T'}^* ∘ g^*`, with the equality realised as the
165	canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors via the pseudo-functoriality
166	of `Scheme.Modules.pullback` (Mathlib `Modules.pullbackComp`).
167	
168	Blueprint reference: `lem:pullback_compose` (Kleiman §2; Stacks tag 01HG). -/
169	
170	/-- **Composition of line-bundle pullbacks** along the projection. For a base
171	scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, test objects `T, T'` over `S`
172	via `πT : T ⟶ S` and `πT' : T' ⟶ S`, and a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`
173	(encoded by the hypothesis `πT' = g ≫ πT`), set
174	`g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T`
175	(the base-change morphism, given by `pullback.map`). On the underlying
176	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` functors the two routes through the canonical
177	naturality square give canonically isomorphic objects:
178	```
179	            Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)
180	  T.Modules  ─────────────────────────────────────────────►  (C ×_S T).Modules
181	       │                                                              │
182	       │ Scheme.Modules.pullback g            Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C
183	       ▼                                                              ▼
184	 T'.Modules  ─────────────────────────────────────────────►  (C ×_S T').Modules
185	            Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')
186	```
187	i.e. for any `N : T.Modules`,
188	```
189	(Modules.pullback g_C).obj ((Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N)
190	  ≅
191	(Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')).obj ((Modules.pullback g).obj N).
192	```
193	
194	This natural isomorphism is the substantive content of the
195	"line-bundle pullback functor is well-behaved under composition" fact
196	(Stacks tag 01HG). At the level of the `OnProduct` carrier (which is a typed
197	`sorry` in iter-174; see §1) it descends to equality of isomorphism classes
198	`[g_C^* π_T^* N] = [π_{T'}^* g^* N] ∈ OnProduct πC πT'`.
199	
200	iter-175+: the body is `Modules.pullbackComp` composed twice (once on each
201	route) and patched together by the equation `πT' = g ≫ πT` (which gives
202	`π_T ∘ g_C = g ∘ π_{T'}` from the pullback square). For the iter-174
203	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
204	theorem pullback_pullback_eq {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
205	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T)
206	    (hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) (N : T.Modules) :
207	    Nonempty
208	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback
209	            (Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
210	              (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg]))).obj
211	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N) ≅
212	        (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT')).obj
213	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj N)) := by
214	  sorry
215	
216	end LineBundle

218	namespace RelPicPresheaf
219	
220	/-! ## §4. The relative Picard quotient (set-valued)
221	
222	The relative Picard presheaf `Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`
223	quotients the line-bundle group of the product by the subgroup pulled back from
224	the base. As a quotient of abelian groups it is a quotient set; equivalently,
225	the equivalence relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` is captured by a
226	`Setoid` on the line-bundle type `OnProduct πC πT`. The quotient
227	`Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` is the underlying set of the relative
228	Picard presheaf at `T`.
229	
230	The "preimage_subgroup" name follows the Kleiman/Stacks convention: the
231	quotient is by the preimage `π_T^* Pic(T) ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T)`, treated as a
232	subgroup (image of a group homomorphism); the project-side Lean encoding
233	extracts the *Setoid* that the quotient construction takes.
234	
235	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined` (Kleiman §2,
236	Def. `df:Pfs`). -/
237	
238	/-- **Well-definedness of the relative Picard quotient as a `Setoid`.**
239	
240	The equivalence relation on `OnProduct πC πT` defining the relative Picard
241	presheaf
242	```
243	Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)
244	```
245	is the relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`. This relation is reflexive
246	(`L ⊗ L⁻¹ = O_(C ×_S T) = π_T^* O_T ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`), symmetric (the inverse of
247	a line bundle in `π_T^* Pic(T)` is in `π_T^* Pic(T)`), and transitive
248	(`L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` and `L' ⊗ L''⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` give
249	`L ⊗ L''⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`); hence is an equivalence relation, encoded by a
250	`Setoid` on `OnProduct πC πT`.
251	
252	The substantive content is the well-definedness of the quotient: the canonical
253	projection `OnProduct πC πT → Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` is the
254	set-theoretic underlying map of the relative Picard presheaf at `T`.
255	
256	iter-175+: the body constructs the `Setoid` from the explicit relation `L ~ L'
257	↔ ∃ N : T.Modules, L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ≅ pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N`. For the
258	iter-174 file-skeleton this is a typed `sorry` because `OnProduct` itself is a
259	typed `sorry` (so the tensor product `L ⊗ L'⁻¹` on the carrier is not yet
260	typed). -/
261	noncomputable def preimage_subgroup {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
262	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) :
263	    Setoid (LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT) :=
264	  sorry
265	
266	/-! ## §5. Naturality in the test scheme — the relative Picard presheaf as a functor
267	
268	For a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S` (encoded by the equality `πT' = g ≫ πT`),
269	the base-change `g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T` induces a map
270	`g_C^* : Pic(C ×_S T) → Pic(C ×_S T')` on line bundles, which (by
271	`lem:pullback_compose` = `pullback_pullback_eq` above) sends the subgroup
272	`π_T^* Pic(T)` into `π_{T'}^* Pic(T')`. Therefore it descends to a unique map
273	on quotients
274	```
275	g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) ⟶ Pic^♯_{C/S}(T'),    [L] ↦ [g_C^* L],
276	```
277	making `Pic^♯_{C/S} : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` a presheaf.
278	
279	For the iter-174 file-skeleton, we pin the **object-level** functorial action
280	(i.e. the induced map of quotient sets for a single test morphism `g`). The
281	full `Functor` packaging (with the identity / composition laws supplied as
282	`map_id`, `map_comp` fields) is iter-175+ work once `OnProduct` and the
283	`preimage_subgroup` carrier are unpacked.
284	
285	Blueprint reference: `thm:pullback_natural` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
286	functor" + Def. `df:Pfs`; Stacks tag 01HG). -/
287	
288	/-- **The relative Picard presheaf is functorial in the test scheme.**
289	
290	Object-level functorial action: for any morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, there
291	is a canonical map
292	```
293	g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) ⟶ Pic^♯_{C/S}(T')
294	```
295	of quotient sets, factoring `g_C^*` through `preimage_subgroup πC πT` and
296	`preimage_subgroup πC πT'`. On representatives, `g^♯ [L] = [g_C^* L]` where
297	`g_C := id_C ×_S g`. The factorisation through the quotient is well-defined
298	because `g_C^*` sends `π_T^* Pic(T) ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T)` into
299	`π_{T'}^* Pic(T') ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T')` (this is the commutative-square content of
300	`pullback_pullback_eq`).
301	
302	iter-175+: the body builds the map via `Quotient.lift` on the lifted `g_C^*`,
303	using the well-definedness from `pullback_pullback_eq`; the identity and
304	composition laws (giving the full `Functor (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` packaging) are
305	the lemmas `id_eq` and `comp_eq` of the quotient-induced map (immediate from
306	`Modules.pullbackId` and `Modules.pullbackComp`). For the iter-174 file-skeleton
307	the body is a typed `sorry`. The signature is non-tautological: it asserts a
308	map between two distinct quotient sets that depends on the morphism `g`. -/
309	noncomputable def functorial {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
310	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T) (_hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) :
311	    Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT) → Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT') :=
312	  sorry
313	
314	end RelPicPresheaf
315	
316	end Scheme

318	end AlgebraicGeometry

1	\chapter{Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-173). This chapter is the A.1.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the line-bundle
7	% pullback functor \(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) along the
8	% projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) of a relative curve, and the
9	% set-valued relative Picard presheaf
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) := \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\)
11	% as a functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). This chapter is gated on
12	% \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (iter-172) for the relative-spectrum machinery
13	% that any concrete Lean-side affine-cover argument will need, and it itself
14	% gates A.1.c (\'etale sheafification / \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a sheaf), A.2.*
15	% (Quot / Hilbert representability), A.3 (identity component), and A.4
16	% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).
17	
18	% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
19	% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a
21	% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). The abelian-group refinement
22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
23	% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
24	% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of \(g^*\) as soon as a
25	% line-bundle pullback functor and its composition identity are in hand, with
26	% no reliance on Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGrp} \'etale-sheafification API which
27	% does not yet exist.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}
87	
88	% NOTE: iter-174 Lane E (file-skeleton) encodes the carrier `OnProduct πC πT`
89	% as `Type (u+1) := sorry` (a TYPE-level scaffold sorry), mirroring the
90	% iter-173 precedent for `Scheme.QcohAlgebra`. Mathlib commit `b80f227`
91	% ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on `Scheme.Modules`; the closest is
92	% `Module.Invertible` (rings-only, in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`).
93	% iter-175+ refines the Lean body to a `structure` pairing a
94	% `(pullback πC πT).Modules` carrier with an invertibility witness (either
95	% Mathlib upstream of an `IsInvertible`-on-sheaf-of-modules predicate, or
96	% a project-side cover-by-affine-opens reduction to `Module.Invertible R`
97	% over each chart).
98	
99	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
100	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
101	
102	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
103	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
104	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
105	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
106	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
107	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
108	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
109	
110	\begin{definition}\leanok
111	[Pullback along the projection]
112	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
114	  
115	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
116	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
117	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
118	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
119	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
120	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
121	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
122	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
123	  %  [\dots]
124	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
125	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
126	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
127	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
128	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
129	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
130	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
131	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
132	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
133	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
134	  in this chapter)
135	  \[
136	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
137	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
138	  \]
139	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
140	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
141	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
142	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
143	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
144	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
145	  the relative Picard presheaf.
146	\end{definition}
147	
148	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
149	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}\leanok
152	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
153	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
154	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
155	  
156	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
157	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
158	  % includes composition)
159	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
160	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
161	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
162	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
163	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
164	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
165	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
166	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
167	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
168	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
169	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
170	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
171	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
172	  canonical projections, and write
173	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
174	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
175	  pullback maps
176	  \[
177	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
178	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
179	  \]
180	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
181	  \[
182	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
183	  \]
184	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
185	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
189	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
190	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
191	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
192	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
193	\begin{proof}
194	  
195	  The square
196	  \[
197	    \begin{array}{ccc}
198	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
199	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
200	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
201	    \end{array}
202	  \]
203	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
204	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
205	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
206	  there is a canonical isomorphism
207	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
208	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
209	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
210	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
211	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
212	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
213	  set-valued maps stated above.
214	\end{proof}
215	
216	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
217	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
218	
219	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
220	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
221	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
222	
223	\begin{theorem}\leanok
224	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
225	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
227	  
228	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
229	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
230	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
231	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
232	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
233	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
234	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
235	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
236	  % topologies  by
237	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
238	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
239	  %  \end{dfn}"
240	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
241	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
242	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
243	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
244	  \[
245	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
246	  \]
247	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
248	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
249	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
250	  a subgroup). Define
251	  \[
252	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
253	  \]
254	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
255	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
256	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
257	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
258	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
259	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
260	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
261	\end{theorem}
262	
263	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
264	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
265	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
266	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
267	% not a translation of an external argument.
268	\begin{proof}
269	  
270	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
271	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
272	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
273	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
274	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
275	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
276	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
277	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
278	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
279	\end{proof}
280	
281	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
282	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
283	
284	\begin{theorem}\leanok
285	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
286	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
288	  
289	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
290	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
291	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
292	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
293	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
294	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
295	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
296	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
297	  %  [\dots]
298	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
299	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
300	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
301	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
302	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
303	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
304	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
305	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
306	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
307	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
308	  \[
309	    \begin{array}{ccc}
310	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
311	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
312	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
313	    \end{array}
314	  \]
315	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
316	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
317	  unique set map
318	  \[
319	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
320	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
321	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
322	  \]
323	  through the quotients of
324	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
325	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
326	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
327	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
328	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
329	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
330	\end{theorem}
331	
332	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
333	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
334	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
335	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
336	\begin{proof}
337	  
338	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
339	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
340	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
341	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
342	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
343	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
344	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
345	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
346	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
347	  universal property of the quotient set
348	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
349	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
350	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
351	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
352	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
353	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
354	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
355	\end{proof}
356	
357	\section{Lean encoding}
358	\label{sec:lbp_lean_encoding}
359	
360	The Lean target file is the new
361	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}, sibling to
362	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
363	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
364	Lean targets as follows.
365	
366	\begin{itemize}
367	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
368	    (\cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}) is a re-export / type alias for the
369	    Mathlib invertible-module predicate on \(C \times_k T\). Mathlib provides
370	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Invertible} (or the equivalent
371	    bundle-of-rank-one packaging in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle});
372	    the project-side declaration simply specialises the Mathlib predicate to
373	    the product scheme.
374	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
375	    (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}) is the pullback map on Picard
376	    groups induced by \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\). The Mathlib backbone is
377	    the pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
378	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback}); the project-side
379	    declaration restricts that pullback to invertible modules and proves
380	    invertibility is preserved (Stacks tag 01HH; one open-cover unfolding).
381	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback\_pullback\_eq}
382	    (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}) is the equality of pullback maps on
383	    isomorphism classes; the Lean form is the propositional equality of two
384	    \texttt{LinearMap}s (or \texttt{Quotient.mk}s) deduced from Mathlib's
385	    pullback-of-modules transitivity.
386	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
387	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is the
388	    structural existence of the quotient set
389	    \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\). Mathlib's quotient-set machinery
390	    (\texttt{Quotient}, \texttt{QuotientGroup}) suffices; the project-side
391	    declaration only packages the universal property in the form
392	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} expects.
393	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
394	    (\cref{thm:pullback_natural}) is the functor instance
395	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\). The naturality square
396	    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
397	    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
398	    quotient-induced map.
399	\end{itemize}
400	
401	No new core axiom is required. The chapter is a definitional / structural
402	construction on top of the existing Mathlib invertible-module + pullback
403	infrastructure, plus the project's relative-spectrum chapter
404	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) for any concrete Lean unfolding that the
405	prover wishes to perform on an affine open of \(C \times_k T\).
406	
407	\section{Out of scope}
408	\label{sec:lbp_out_of_scope}
409	
410	This chapter packages \emph{only} the line-bundle pullback functor on a
411	relative curve and the well-definedness + functoriality of the set-valued
412	quotient \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The following downstream constructions live in
413	separate sibling chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
414	\begin{itemize}
415	  \item \textbf{Group structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)} (A.1.c) --- the
416	    quotient is here treated as set-valued; the abelian-group enhancement
417	    (and the \'etale-sheafification step that turns the presheaf into a
418	    representable functor) belongs to the planned chapter
419	    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex}.
420	  \item \textbf{Comparison theorem $\Pic_{X/S} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{(X/S)\zar}
421	    \hookrightarrow \Pic_{(X/S)\et} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$}
422	    (Kleiman §2 Thm~\texttt{th:cmp}) --- out of scope here (\'etale-sheaf
423	    refinement; A.1.c).
424	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) as a scheme} --- A.1.c +
425	    A.2.* (Hilbert / Quot schemes); requires the flattening-stratification
426	    chapter \texttt{Picard\_FGA\_FlatteningStratification.tex} (planned, not
427	    yet on disk; do not forward-reference).
428	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and abelian-variety
429	    structure} --- A.3 (out of scope; depends on \'etale-sheaf and Hilbert
430	    machinery).
431	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property / Jacobian morphism} --- A.4 (out
432	    of scope; consumes the entire Route~A stack).
433	\end{itemize}
434

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Abelian
9	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Colimits
10	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PullbackContinuous
11	public import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Presheaf
12	public import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.OpenImmersion
13	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Bicategory.Adjunction.Adj
14	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Bicategory.Adjunction.Cat
15	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Bicategory.Functor.LocallyDiscrete
16	
17	/-!
18	# The category of sheaves of modules over a scheme
19	
20	In this file, we define the abelian category of sheaves of modules
21	`X.Modules` over a scheme `X`, and study its basic functoriality.
22	
23	-/
24	
25	@[expose] public section
26	
27	universe t u
28	
29	open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace SheafOfModules Bicategory
30	
31	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
32	
33	variable {X Y Z T : Scheme.{u}}
34	
35	variable (X) in
36	/-- The category of sheaves of modules over a scheme. -/
37	def Modules := SheafOfModules.{u} X.ringCatSheaf
38	
39	namespace Modules
40	
41	/-- Morphisms between `𝒪ₓ`-modules. Use `Hom.app` to act on sections. -/
42	def Hom (M N : X.Modules) : Type u := SheafOfModules.Hom M N
43	
44	instance : Category X.Modules where
45	  Hom := Modules.Hom
46	  __ := (inferInstance : Category (SheafOfModules.{u} X.ringCatSheaf))
47	
48	noncomputable instance : Abelian X.Modules :=
49	  inferInstanceAs <| Abelian (SheafOfModules.{u} X.ringCatSheaf)
50	instance : HasLimits X.Modules := inferInstanceAs (HasLimits (SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf))
51	instance : HasColimits X.Modules := inferInstanceAs (HasColimits (SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf))
52	
53	section Functor
54	
55	variable (X) in
56	/-- The forgetful functor from `𝒪ₓ`-modules to presheaves of modules.
57	This is mostly useful to transport results from (pre)sheaves of modules to `𝒪ₓ`-modules and
58	usually shouldn't be used directly when working with actual `𝒪ₓ`-modules. -/
59	def toPresheafOfModules : X.Modules ⥤ X.PresheafOfModules := SheafOfModules.forget _
60	
61	/-- The forgetful functor from `𝒪ₓ`-modules to presheaves of modules is fully faithful. -/
62	def fullyFaithfulToPresheafOfModules : (Modules.toPresheafOfModules X).FullyFaithful :=
63	  SheafOfModules.fullyFaithfulForget _
64	
65	instance : (toPresheafOfModules X).Full := fullyFaithfulToPresheafOfModules.full
66	instance : (toPresheafOfModules X).Faithful := fullyFaithfulToPresheafOfModules.faithful
67	instance : (toPresheafOfModules X).IsRightAdjoint :=
68	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).isRightAdjoint
69	
70	variable (X) in
71	/-- The forgetful functor from `𝒪ₓ`-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. -/
72	noncomputable def toPresheaf : X.Modules ⥤ TopCat.Presheaf Ab X :=
73	  toPresheafOfModules X ⋙ PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _
74	
75	instance : (toPresheaf X).Faithful := .comp _ (PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _)
76	instance : PreservesLimits (toPresheaf X) := comp_preservesLimits _ (PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _)
77	instance : (toPresheaf X).ReflectsIsomorphisms :=
78	  reflectsIsomorphisms_comp _ (PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _)
79	
80	end Functor
81	
82	variable {M N K : X.Modules} {φ : M ⟶ N} {U V : X.Opens}
83	
84	section Presheaf
85	
86	/-- The underlying abelian presheaf of an `𝒪ₓ`-module. -/
87	noncomputable def presheaf (M : X.Modules) : TopCat.Presheaf Ab X := M.1.presheaf
88	
89	/-- Notation for sections of a presheaf of module. -/
90	scoped[AlgebraicGeometry] notation3 "Γ(" M ", " U ")" => (Scheme.Modules.presheaf M).obj (.op U)
91	
92	instance : Module Γ(X, U) Γ(M, U) := (M.val.obj (.op U)).isModule
93	
94	variable (M) in
95	@[simp] lemma map_smul (i : U ⟶ V) (r : Γ(X, V)) (x : Γ(M, V)) :
96	    M.presheaf.map i.op (r • x) = X.presheaf.map i.op r • M.presheaf.map i.op x :=
97	  M.val.map_smul _ _ _
98	
99	/-- The underlying map between abelian presheaves of a morphism of `𝒪ₓ`-modules. -/
100	noncomputable def Hom.mapPresheaf (φ : M ⟶ N) : M.presheaf ⟶ N.presheaf :=
101	  (toPresheaf X).map φ
102	
103	/-- The application of a morphism of `𝒪ₓ`-modules to sections. -/
104	def Hom.app (φ : M ⟶ N) (U : X.Opens) : Γ(M, U) ⟶ Γ(N, U) :=
105	  (forget₂ _ _).map (φ.val.app (.op U))
106	
107	@[simp] lemma mapPresheaf_app (φ : M ⟶ N) (U) : φ.mapPresheaf.app U = φ.app U.unop := rfl
108	
109	@[simp]
110	lemma Hom.app_smul (φ : M ⟶ N) (r : Γ(X, U)) (x : Γ(M, U)) :
111	    φ.app U (r • x) = r • φ.app U x :=
112	  (φ.val.app (.op U)).hom.map_smul r x
113	
114	@[simp] lemma Hom.add_app (φ ψ : M ⟶ N) : (φ + ψ).app U = φ.app U + ψ.app U := rfl
115	@[simp] lemma Hom.sub_app (φ ψ : M ⟶ N) : (φ - ψ).app U = φ.app U - ψ.app U := rfl
116	@[simp] lemma Hom.zero_app : (0 : M ⟶ N).app U = 0 := rfl
117	@[simp] lemma Hom.id_app (M : X.Modules) : (𝟙 M :).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
118	@[simp] lemma Hom.comp_app (φ : M ⟶ N) (ψ : N ⟶ K) : (φ ≫ ψ).app U = φ.app U ≫ ψ.app U := rfl
119	
120	@[ext]
121	lemma hom_ext (f g : M ⟶ N) (H : ∀ U, f.app U = g.app U) : f = g := by
122	  apply SheafOfModules.hom_ext
123	  ext U x
124	  exact congr($(H U.unop) x)
125	
126	lemma isSheaf (M : X.Modules) : M.presheaf.IsSheaf := SheafOfModules.isSheaf M
127	
128	@[simp] lemma toPresheaf_obj : (toPresheaf X).obj M = M.presheaf := rfl
129	@[simp] lemma toPresheaf_map : (toPresheaf X).map φ = φ.mapPresheaf := rfl
130	
131	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
132	lemma Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app {M N : X.Modules} {φ : M ⟶ N} :
133	    IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U, IsIso (φ.app U) := by
134	  rw [← isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso _ (toPresheaf X), NatTrans.isIso_iff_isIso_app]
135	  simp [Opposite.op_surjective.forall]
136	
137	instance [IsIso φ] : IsIso (φ.app U) := Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app.mp ‹_› _
138	
139	@[simp, push ←]
140	lemma inv_app [IsIso φ] : (inv φ).app U = inv (φ.app U) := by
141	  apply IsIso.eq_inv_of_hom_inv_id
142	  simp [← Hom.comp_app]
143	
144	end Presheaf
145	
146	noncomputable section Functorial
147	
148	variable (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) (h : Z ⟶ T)
149	
150	/-- The pushforward functor for categories of sheaves of modules over schemes. -/
151	def pushforward : X.Modules ⥤ Y.Modules :=
152	  SheafOfModules.pushforward f.toRingCatSheafHom
153	
154	@[simp]
155	lemma pushforward_obj_obj (M : X.Modules) (U : Y.Opens) :
156	    Γ((pushforward f).obj M, U) = Γ(M, f ⁻¹ᵁ U) := rfl
157	
158	@[simp]
159	lemma pushforward_obj_presheaf_map {U V : Y.Opens} (i : U ⟶ V) :
160	    ((pushforward f).obj M).presheaf.map i.op = M.presheaf.map ((Opens.map f.base).map i).op := rfl
161	
162	@[simp]
163	lemma pushforward_map_app (φ : M ⟶ N) (U : Y.Opens) :
164	    ((pushforward f).map φ).app U = φ.app (f ⁻¹ᵁ U) := rfl
165	
166	/-- The pullback functor for categories of sheaves of modules over schemes. -/
167	def pullback : Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules :=
168	  SheafOfModules.pullback f.toRingCatSheafHom
169	
170	/-- The pullback functor for categories of sheaves of modules over schemes
171	is left adjoint to the pushforward functor. -/
172	def pullbackPushforwardAdjunction : pullback f ⊣ pushforward f :=
173	  SheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction _
174	
175	section
176	
177	attribute [local instance] preservesBinaryBiproducts_of_preservesBinaryCoproducts
178	  preservesBinaryBiproducts_of_preservesBinaryProducts
179	
180	instance : (pullback f).IsLeftAdjoint := (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).isLeftAdjoint
181	instance : (pushforward f).IsRightAdjoint := (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).isRightAdjoint
182	instance : (pushforward f).Additive := Functor.additive_of_preservesBinaryBiproducts _
183	instance : (pullback f).Additive := Functor.additive_of_preservesBinaryBiproducts _
184	
185	end
186	
187	variable (X) in
188	/-- The pushforward of sheaves of modules by the identity morphism identifies
189	to the identity functor. -/
190	def pushforwardId : pushforward (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
191	  SheafOfModules.pushforwardId _
192	
193	@[simp] lemma pushforwardId_hom_app_app : ((pushforwardId X).hom.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
194	@[simp] lemma pushforwardId_inv_app_app : ((pushforwardId X).inv.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
195	
196	variable (X) in
197	/-- The pullback of sheaves of modules by the identity morphism identifies
198	to the identity functor. -/
199	def pullbackId : pullback (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
200	  SheafOfModules.pullbackId _
201	
202	variable (X) in
203	lemma conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom :
204	    conjugateEquiv .id (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 X)) (pullbackId X).hom =
205	      (pushforwardId X).inv :=
206	  SheafOfModules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom _
207	
208	/-- The composition of two pushforward functors for sheaves of modules on schemes
209	identify to the pushforward for the composition. -/
210	def pushforwardComp :
211	    pushforward f ⋙ pushforward g ≅ pushforward (f ≫ g) :=
212	  SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp _ _
213	
214	@[simp] lemma pushforwardComp_hom_app_app (U) : ((pushforwardComp f g).hom.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
215	@[simp] lemma pushforwardComp_inv_app_app (U) : ((pushforwardComp f g).inv.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
216	
217	/-- The composition of two pullback functors for sheaves of modules on schemes
218	identify to the pullback for the composition. -/
219	def pullbackComp :
220	    pullback g ⋙ pullback f ≅ pullback (f ≫ g) :=
221	  SheafOfModules.pullbackComp _ _
222	
223	/-- Pushforwards along equal morphisms are isomorphic. -/
224	def pushforwardCongr {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) : pushforward f ≅ pushforward g :=
225	    pushforwardNatIso _ (Opens.mapIso _ _ (hf ▸ rfl)) ≪≫
226	      SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr (by cat_disch)
227	
228	@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_hom_app_app {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) (U : Y.Opens) :
229	    ((pushforwardCongr hf).hom.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (hf ▸ rfl)).op := rfl
230	
231	@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_inv_app_app {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) (U : Y.Opens) :
232	    ((pushforwardCongr hf).inv.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (hf ▸ rfl)).op := rfl
233	
234	/-- Inverse images along equal morphisms are isomorphic. -/
235	def pullbackCongr {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) : pullback f ≅ pullback g :=
236	  eqToIso (hf ▸ rfl)
237	
238	lemma conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv :
239	    conjugateEquiv ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).comp (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
240	      (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ g)) (pullbackComp f g).inv =
241	    (pushforwardComp f g).hom :=
242	  SheafOfModules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv _ _
243	
244	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
245	@[reassoc]
246	lemma pseudofunctor_associativity :
247	    (pullbackComp f (g ≫ h)).inv ≫
248	      Functor.whiskerRight (pullbackComp g h).inv _ ≫ (Functor.associator _ _ _).hom ≫

/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: invertible sheaves of
`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
Picard functor at `T`.

iter-175+: instantiate as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules` carrying an
invertibility witness — concretely either
```
structure OnProduct ... where
  sheaf : (pullback πC πT).Modules
  isInvertible : IsInvertible sheaf
```
or the quotient by isomorphism for the Picard *group* refinement. For the
iter-174 file-skeleton this is a typed `sorry` at the type level because
Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on `Scheme.Modules`. -/
noncomputable def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (_πC : C ⟶ S) (_πT : T ⟶ S) :
    Type (u+1) :=
  sorry
input.new_string:
/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: sheaves of
`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
Picard functor at `T`.

iter-186: instantiated as `(Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules`, i.e. the
category of all `O`-modules on the product. This is a deliberate
simplification: Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on
`Scheme.Modules`, so we forget the invertibility constraint at the carrier
level — the iso-class setoid + the quotient-set definition in §4 still
produces a meaningful set-valued presheaf. iter-187+: refine by pairing
the carrier with an invertibility witness (project-side predicate via
local trivialisation on an affine open cover, or upstream Mathlib once an
`IsInvertible`-on-`Scheme.Modules` predicate is added). -/
@[reducible] noncomputable def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (πC : C ⟶ S)
    (πT : T ⟶ S) : Type (u+1) :=
  (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules

/-- **Pullback along the projection** `π_T : C ×_S T ⟶ T` of a relative
curve. Sends an `O_T`-line bundle `N` to the `O_(C ×_S T)`-line bundle
`π_T^* N`, induced by the underlying `Scheme.Modules.pullback` functor on
`Scheme.Modules`.

iter-175+: instantiate as `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`
wrapped with the carried `IsInvertible` witness (the pullback of an invertible
sheaf is invertible, Stacks 01HH). For the iter-174 file-skeleton the body is
a typed `sorry` because the carrier type `OnProduct` is itself a typed `sorry`
(see §1). The signature is non-tautological: it asserts a *function*
`T.Modules → OnProduct πC πT`, which after unfolding `OnProduct` becomes the
concrete pullback functor restricted to invertible modules. -/
noncomputable def pullbackAlongProjection {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (_N : T.Modules) : OnProduct πC πT :=
  sorry
input.new_string:
/-- **Pullback along the projection** `π_T : C ×_S T ⟶ T` of a relative
curve. Sends an `O_T`-module `N` to the `O_(C ×_S T)`-module
`π_T^* N`, induced by the `Scheme.Modules.pullback` functor on
`Scheme.Modules`.

iter-186: with `OnProduct := (pullback πC πT).Modules`, the body is the
direct application of Mathlib's `Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)`
functor at `N`. The invertibility-preservation step (Stacks 01HH) is
deferred to iter-187+ once `OnProduct` carries an invertibility witness. -/
noncomputable def pullbackAlongProjection {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (N : T.Modules) : OnProduct πC πT :=
  (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N

216	/-! ## §4. The relative Picard quotient (set-valued)
217	
218	The relative Picard presheaf `Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`
219	quotients the line-bundle group of the product by the subgroup pulled back from
220	the base. As a quotient of abelian groups it is a quotient set; equivalently,
221	the equivalence relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` is captured by a
222	`Setoid` on the line-bundle type `OnProduct πC πT`. The quotient
223	`Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` is the underlying set of the relative
224	Picard presheaf at `T`.

theorem pullback_pullback_eq {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}

iter-186: closed via the chain
1. `Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g_C (pullback.snd πC πT)` (functor iso applied at `N`)
2. `Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr` for the equation `g_C ≫ snd πC πT = snd πC πT' ≫ g`
   (`pullback.lift_snd` on the `pullback.map` lift).
3. `(Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (pullback.snd πC πT') g).symm` (applied at `N`). -/
theorem pullback_pullback_eq {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T)
    (hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) (N : T.Modules) :
    Nonempty
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback
            (Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
              (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg]))).obj
          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N) ≅
        (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT')).obj
          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj N)) := by
  set g_C : Limits.pullback πC πT' ⟶ Limits.pullback πC πT :=
    Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
      (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg]) with hg_C
  have key : g_C ≫ Limits.pullback.snd πC πT = Limits.pullback.snd πC πT' ≫ g := by
    simp [g_C]
  refine ⟨?_⟩
  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g_C (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).app N ≪≫ ?_
  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr key).app N ≪≫ ?_
  exact ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT') g).app N).symm
